name: Baalbek Platform
description: The Baalbek Platform refers to the massive stone foundation upon which the Roman Temple of Jupiter stands in Baalbek, Lebanon. While mainstream archaeology attributes the platform's construction to the Roman Empire, particularly during the reigns of emperors Augustus and Nero, alternative theories propose that it predates the Romans by millennia, potentially built by a lost civilization or even extraterrestrial beings due to the immense size and precise fitting of the megalithic stones, especially the 'Trilithon' (three stones weighing approximately 800 tons each). Proponents point to the lack of definitive Roman records detailing the platform's construction, the sheer scale of the stones which seems beyond Roman engineering capabilities (especially considering the lack of iron tools capable of handling such weight), and the existence of even larger, unfinished stones in a nearby quarry as evidence of a pre-Roman origin. Mainstream archaeology maintains that the Romans were capable of such feats, utilizing advanced (for their time) engineering techniques, slave labor, and a sophisticated understanding of logistics. They explain the unfinished stones in the quarry as evidence of Roman planning and abandonment due to logistical challenges or political upheaval. The smooth fitting of stones is attributed to Roman precision and mortar. Dating the platform remains challenging due to the difficulty of obtaining reliable samples from within the structure itself. The debate centers on whether the platform represents Roman engineering prowess or evidence of a more ancient, advanced civilization.
subtitle: Megalithic marvel or Roman ingenuity?

claims.claim: The Baalbek platform predates the Romans by millennia.
claims.evidence: Scale of the stones, lack of detailed Roman construction records, unfinished stones in the quarry.
claims.criticism: Mainstream archaeology attributes it to Roman engineering and planning, citing logistical challenges as reason for unfinished stones.

claims.claim: The Trilithon stones are too heavy for Roman technology to have moved and placed.
claims.evidence: Weight of the stones (800 tons each), lack of iron tools capable of handling such weight.
claims.criticism: Romans used ramps, levers, and manpower, and the stones were likely moved incrementally over long periods. Iron tools existed, albeit perhaps not as advanced as modern tools, but sufficient for basic quarrying.

claims.claim: A lost civilization or extraterrestrial beings built the platform.
claims.evidence: Precise fitting of stones, advanced engineering seemingly beyond Roman capabilities.
claims.criticism: The fitting is impressive but not necessarily beyond Roman capabilities. Mortar and precise measurement techniques were used. Attributing it to lost civilizations or extraterrestrials lacks supporting evidence.

keyFigures.name: Graham Hancock
keyFigures.role: Promoter of the pre-Roman origin theory.

keyFigures.name: Robert Temple
keyFigures.role: Author proposing pre-Roman and potentially extraterrestrial involvement.

keyFigures.name: Jeanine Abdul Massih
keyFigures.role: Lebanese archaeologist who suggests the site was a Roman construction, dismissing claims of pre-Roman origins and advanced technology.

mythologyConnections: Possible connections to earlier Canaanite deities

timeline.event: Hypothetical construction by a lost civilization (according to alternative theories).
Construction of the Temple of Jupiter by the Romans, incorporating the existing platform.
Increased interest in the platform and the emergence of alternative theories regarding its origin.

sources.author: David Macaulay
sources.work: City: A Story of Roman Planning and Construction
sources.title: David Macaulay
